key_insight: Uniformly accurate surrogate learning on the Boolean hypercube is much harder than standard average-error learning: low-degree polynomials need Θ(n^{d+1}) samples and s-sparse Fourier-Walsh polynomials need Θ(ns^2) samples under subgaussian noise.
engineering_relevance: For optimization pipelines that rely on surrogate models over binary decision variables, these tight L∞ bounds tell you how many noisy samples are needed to preserve solution quality when optimizing the surrogate.
method: Derive minimax sample-complexity bounds for bounded Boolean polynomials under noisy observations.
Separate results for degree-≤d polynomials and s-sparse Fourier-Walsh polynomials.
Use auxiliary norms to control uniform L∞ error where standard Fourier/L2 tools are insufficient.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports tight lower and upper bounds, including impossibility results that hold even for adaptive learners, showing the rates are intrinsic to the noisy setting.
watch_item: The guarantees are for subgaussian noise and specific polynomial/sparsity classes, so implementation must check that the target surrogate and noise model fit these assumptions.
paper_title: Tight $L_\infty$ Sample Complexity for Low-Degree and Sparse Boolean Polynomials
